EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second winter storm of the season was also a mixed precipitation event.  A "hybrid" surface low pressure system moved nearly straight east across the northern plains.  It tracked across northern South Dakota to Aberdeen, then toward Bemidji (MN) and Duluth.  A strong thermal gradient developed during the day on thursday (30th), with highs from around 20 near Cando, ND, to around 40 from Forman, ND, to Parkers Prairie, MN.  A swath of freezing rain fell first, coating many areas with a quarter inch of ice.  As the temperature fell, the freezing rain changed to snow.  The most snow, 6 to 10 inches, fell along the canadian border from Langdon to Pembina (ND) to Hallock to Roseau (MN).  Less snow fell south of this area, roughly 2 to 5 inches along the U.S. Highway 2 corridor.  Wind speeds did pick up as the low pressure system moved east, producing some visibility restrictions in blowing snow.  Quite a bit more snow fell north of the international border, prompting the closure of Manitoba Highways 75 and 59 north of the Pembina (ND) and Lancaster (MN) border crossings.  This stranded many Canadian hockey fans in Grand Forks (ND) and Thief River Falls (MN), who had been watching the men's (under age 20)  International Hockey Federation tournament.

Freezing rain caused ice up to one-half inch thick to accumulate on roads, sidewalks, trees and power lines. There were many reports of tree damage and sporadic power outages.

A "hybrid" surface low pressure system moved nearly straight east across the northern plains.  It tracked across northern South Dakota to Aberdeen, then toward Bemidji (MN) and Duluth.  A strong thermal gradient developed during the day on thursday (30th), with highs from around 20 near Cando, ND, to around 40 from Forman, ND, to Parkers Prairie, MN.  An area of rain over eastern South Dakota moved into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota and fell as freezing rain.  A good quarter to half inch of ice accumulated across the area.  As temperatures rose to the melting point or above by thursday afternoon, some of the ice melted.  This prevented widespread power outages and major travel problems.  Even so, quite a few accidents were reported.  As the low pressure system moved east, temperatures fell thursday night.  Any slush or wet spots quickly froze, making travel hazardous once again.

Low pressure that formed over the Southern Great Plains moved across southeast Minnesota early on the 26th. This storm dropped moderate to heavy snow across central and south central Minnesota between late afternoon on the 24th and early afternoon on the 26th.  Snowfall totals ranged from 18 to 20 inches in Todd County and northern Morrison County to around 6 inches in an area extending from Redwood Falls to the western Twin Cities suburbs (see attached graphic). The highest totals included 18 to 20 inches in Staples, 14 inches in Little Falls, 11 inches in Onamia, and 10 inches in Alexandria, Milaca, and Long Prairie. Four to 5 inches of snow fell south and east of the Redwood Falls to Twin Cities line but wind gusts to 25 MPH during this storm caused low visibility.

Low pressure that formed over the Southern Great Plains moved across southeast Minnesota early on the 26th. This storm dropped moderate to heavy snow across central and south central Minnesota between late afternoon on the 24th and early afternoon on the 26th.  Snowfall totals ranged from 18 to 20 inches in Todd County and northern Morrison County to around 6 inches in an area extending from Redwood Falls to the western Twin Cities suburbs (see attached graphic). The highest totals included 18 to 20 inches in Staples, 14 inches in Little Falls, 11 inches in Onamia, and 10 inches in Alexandria, Milaca, and Long Prairie. Four to 5 inches of snow fell south and east of the Redwood Falls to Twin Cities line but wind gusts to 25 MPH during this storm caused low visibility.

Snowfall of 5 to 10 inches was accompanied by winds strong enough to reduce visibilities to a quarter mile or less at times in blowing snow. The winds also caused drifting snow, which contributed to making travel very difficult. In addition, dangerous wind chills developed late in the storm as actual temperatures fell. Numerous schools were closed and many school related activities were cancelled.

A strong cold front pushed through Minnesota during the early morning hours of December 12th.  By dawn, winds turned to the northwest and increased to 25 to 40 MPH with gusts as high as 70 MPH.  The windiest part of the day was from mid morning through mid afternoon when many locations suffered sustained winds in the 30 to 45 MPH range.  The highest wind gusts recorded in southern Minnesota during this time included 71 MPH in Welch and 62 MPH near Albert Lea, St. James, Winthrop and Owatonna.  Other notable wind gusts included 59 MPH at New Ulm,  58 MPH in Mankato, 55 MPH in St. Cloud and Morris, 54 MPH at Redwood Falls, and 52 MPH at the Minneapolis/St. Paul International Airport.  Scattered trees were downed and a few buildings received minor roof damage across the region.

The highest totals included 18 to 20 inches in Staples, 14 inches in Little Falls, 11 inches in Onamia, and 10 inches in Alexandria, Milaca, and Long Prairie. Four to 5 inches of snow fell south and east of the Redwood Falls to Twin Cities line but wind gusts to 25 MPH during this storm caused low visibility.

A strong cold front pushed through Minnesota during the early morning hours of December 12th.  By dawn, winds turned to the northwest and increased to 25 to 40 MPH with gusts as high as 70 MPH.  The windiest part of the day was from mid morning through mid afternoon when many locations suffered sustained winds in the 30 to 45 MPH range.  The highest wind gusts recorded in southern Minnesota during this time included 71 MPH in Welch and 62 MPH near Albert Lea, St. James, Winthrop and Owatonna.  Other notable wind gusts included 59 MPH at New Ulm,  58 MPH in Mankato, 55 MPH in St. Cloud and Morris, 54 MPH at Redwood Falls, and 52 MPH at the Minneapolis/St. Paul International Airport.  Scattered trees were downed and a few buildings received minor roof damage across the region.

The second winter storm of the season was also a mixed precipitation event.  A "hybrid" surface low pressure system moved nearly straight east across the northern plains.  It tracked across northern South Dakota to Aberdeen, then toward Bemidji (MN) and Duluth.  A strong thermal gradient developed during the day on thursday (30th), with highs from around 20 near Cando, ND, to around 40 from Forman, ND, to Parkers Prairie, MN.  A swath of freezing rain fell first, coating many areas with a quarter inch of ice.  As the temperature fell, the freezing rain changed to snow.  The most snow, 6 to 10 inches, fell along the canadian border from Langdon to Pembina (ND) to Hallock to Roseau (MN).  Less snow fell south of this area, roughly 2 to 5 inches along the U.S. Highway 2 corridor.  Wind speeds did pick up as the low pressure system moved east, producing some visibility restrictions in blowing snow.  Quite a bit more snow fell north of the international border, prompting the closure of Manitoba Highways 75 and 59 north of the Pembina (ND) and Lancaster (MN) border crossings.  This stranded many Canadian hockey fans in Grand Forks (ND) and Thief River Falls (MN), who had been watching the men's (under age 20)  International Hockey Federation tournament.

A "hybrid" surface low pressure system moved nearly straight east across the northern plains.  It tracked across northern South Dakota to Aberdeen, then toward Bemidji (MN) and Duluth.  A strong thermal gradient developed during the day on thursday (30th), with highs from around 20 near Cando, ND, to around 40 from Forman, ND, to Parkers Prairie, MN.  An area of rain over eastern South Dakota moved into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota and fell as freezing rain.  A good quarter to half inch of ice accumulated across the area.  As temperatures rose to the melting point or above by thursday afternoon, some of the ice melted.  This prevented widespread power outages and major travel problems.  Even so, quite a few accidents were reported.  As the low pressure system moved east, temperatures fell thursday night.  Any slush or wet spots quickly froze, making travel hazardous once again.

Other notable wind gusts included 59 MPH at New Ulm,  58 MPH in Mankato, 55 MPH in St. Cloud and Morris, 54 MPH at Redwood Falls, and 52 MPH at the Minneapolis/St. Paul International Airport.  Scattered trees were downed and a few buildings received minor roof damage across the region.

The second winter storm of the season was also a mixed precipitation event.  A "hybrid" surface low pressure system moved nearly straight east across the northern plains.  It tracked across northern South Dakota to Aberdeen, then toward Bemidji (MN) and Duluth.  A strong thermal gradient developed during the day on thursday (30th), with highs from around 20 near Cando, ND, to around 40 from Forman, ND, to Parkers Prairie, MN.  A swath of freezing rain fell first, coating many areas with a quarter inch of ice.  As the temperature fell, the freezing rain changed to snow.  The most snow, 6 to 10 inches, fell along the canadian border from Langdon to Pembina (ND) to Hallock to Roseau (MN).  Less snow fell south of this area, roughly 2 to 5 inches along the U.S. Highway 2 corridor.  Wind speeds did pick up as the low pressure system moved east, producing some visibility restrictions in blowing snow.  Quite a bit more snow fell north of the international border, prompting the closure of Manitoba Highways 75 and 59 north of the Pembina (ND) and Lancaster (MN) border crossings.  This stranded many Canadian hockey fans in Grand Forks (ND) and Thief River Falls (MN), who had been watching the men's (under age 20)  International Hockey Federation tournament.

A "hybrid" surface low pressure system moved nearly straight east across the northern plains.  It tracked across northern South Dakota to Aberdeen, then toward Bemidji (MN) and Duluth.  A strong thermal gradient developed during the day on thursday (30th), with highs from around 20 near Cando, ND, to around 40 from Forman, ND, to Parkers Prairie, MN.  An area of rain over eastern South Dakota moved into southeast North Dakota and west central Minnesota and fell as freezing rain.  A good quarter to half inch of ice accumulated across the area.  As temperatures rose to the melting point or above by thursday afternoon, some of the ice melted.  This prevented widespread power outages and major travel problems.  Even so, quite a few accidents were reported.  As the low pressure system moved east, temperatures fell thursday night.  Any slush or wet spots quickly froze, making travel hazardous once again.

Low pressure that formed over the Southern Great Plains moved across southeast Minnesota early on the 26th. This storm dropped moderate to heavy snow across central and south central Minnesota between late afternoon on the 24th and early afternoon on the 26th.  Snowfall totals ranged from 18 to 20 inches in Todd County and northern Morrison County to around 6 inches in an area extending from Redwood Falls to the western Twin Cities suburbs (see attached graphic). The highest totals included 18 to 20 inches in Staples, 14 inches in Little Falls, 11 inches in Onamia, and 10 inches in Alexandria, Milaca, and Long Prairie. Four to 5 inches of snow fell south and east of the Redwood Falls to Twin Cities line but wind gusts to 25 MPH during this storm caused low visibility.

Snowfall of 5 to 10 inches was accompanied by winds strong enough to reduce visibilities to a quarter mile or less at times in blowing snow. The winds also caused drifting snow, which contributed to making travel very difficult. In addition, dangerous wind chills developed late in the storm as actual temperatures fell. Numerous schools were closed and many school related activities were cancelled.

High pressure extended from eastern Montana into central South Dakota, with a decent surface pressure gradient over northwest Minnesota.  This kept a steady west to northwest wind at 10 to 20 mph and wind chills from 40 below to 60 degrees below zero over portions of northwest Minnesota.  The coldest morning lows on monday (January 5th) were 32 below at Baudette and 29 below at Humboldt.

Heavy snow of 6 to 10 inches fell across Big Stone and Traverse counties. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Artichoke Lake and Ortonville, 7 inches at Browns Valley, 9 inches at Tintah, and 10 inches at Wheaton.

Heavy snow fell over much of northeastern Minnesota. The heavy snow area of eight or more inches was in an eighty-mile-wind swath from Pillager in southern Cass County to the tip of the Arrowhead. The most snow fell a few miles inland from Lake Superior from Duluth up the north shore where amounts ranged from a foot to just over 30 inches. The Duluth NWS had 27.1 inches, Finland DNR had 26 inches, Adolph had 25 inches while a spotter 2 miles east of Wales (southern Lake County) had 21 inches.

Roseau had the coldest morning temperature (on the 22nd), at 22 below zero.

An Alberta Clipper tracked from southeast Saskatchewan to Fargo, and then to the Minneapolis area.  Light snow fell along and north of this low track.  Most of the snow fell in the warm air ahead of the low.  Angle Inlet, in northwest Lake of the Woods county, reported 6 inches of snow.

Six to 10.5 inches of snow fell across the Minnesota Arrowhead. The highest amounts were just inland from Lake Superior. Finland had 10". International Falls set a new record for daily snowfall after receiving 5.5 inches. They ended up with a total of 8 inches.

Heavy snow fell over much of northeastern Minnesota. The heavy snow area of eight or more inches was in an eighty-mile-wind swath from Pillager in southern Cass County to the tip of the Arrowhead. The most snow fell a few miles inland from Lake Superior from Duluth up the north shore where amounts ranged from a foot to just over 30 inches. The Duluth NWS had 27.1 inches, Finland DNR had 26 inches, Adolph had 25 inches while a spotter 2 miles east of Wales (southern Lake County) had 21 inches.

A surface low over western South Dakota friday morning tracked into eastern South Dakota by late friday morning.  It then tracked northeast along its inverted trough, roughly from Wahpeton, ND, at noon, to Lake of the Woods, MN, by early evening.  Arctic high pressure built in quickly behind the departing low, creating a tight surface pressure gradient and gusty northwest winds.  Wind speeds were steady at 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 35 mph.  Wind speeds were a bit higher across the extreme northern Red River Valley.  At Hallock, MN, winds gusted up to 45 mph.  Blowing snow resulted in visibilities below a half-mile in open areas, and near zero at times in the northern Red River Valley.  Eight inches of snow fell across portions of Roseau, East Marshall, and Northern Beltrami counties, with 4 to 6 inches elsewhere.  Several schools cancelled classes and evening activities on friday.

Arctic high pressure built into the northern plains in the wake of the January 24th-26th snowstorm, which dropped heavy snow over all of northwest Minnesota.  With subzero temperatures and wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph, wind chills dropped to 40 below to 65 below zero on the afternoon of the 27th.  Therefore, a wind chill warning was issued from the afternoon of the 27th through the afternoon of the 28th.  Dalton, MN, reported a wind chill of 62 below zero.  Wind speeds dropped off enough that the wind chill warning was not extended beyond the afternoon of the 28th.  However, with the arctic high pressure over the area, extremely cold temperatures abounded.  On the morning of the 27th, Perley fell to 36 below zero.  The coldest morning occurred on the 30th, when the temperature at Fosston fell to 50 degrees below zero, Ada fell to 48 below, Park Rapids fell to 45 below, and Thorhult fell to 43 below.  These were the coldest temperatures recorded during this stretch of subzero weather.  On the morning of the 31st, both Perley and Roseau reported lows of 34 degrees below zero.  Auto repair shops were kept busy jumping stalled cars, installing block heaters, repairing flat tires, and fixing batteries and spark plugs.  Residents around Moran Lake and Two Inlets Lake (near Park Rapids) reported brief power outages.

Low pressure over Nebraska moved across southern Minnesota during the morning of the 1st bringing a few inches of snowfall accumulation. A second, more powerful storm tracked from Missouri into Illinois early on the 2nd. The second storm dropped moderate to heavy snow across east central and south central Minnesota between the evening of the 1st and the evening of the 2nd. Snowfall totals from both storms ranged from 6 inches along a line from Redwood Falls to Mora to a foot across south central Minnesota (see attached graphic). The highest totals included 13 inches in Faribault and 12 inches at St. James, Albert Lea, and Blue Earth. Behind the second system, temperatures plummeted into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills dropped into the 30s below zero on the morning of the 3rd.

An inverted trough extended into the northern plains from an area of surface low pressure over the central plains.  Much of the area received over 6 inches of snow.  A heavy snow band set up from Cooperstown to Mayville (North Dakota) and toward Wadena, Minnesota.  Up to 2 feet of snow fell along this line.  Wind chills of 20 below to 30 below zero also occurred during the day sunday (January 25th).  17 inches of snow fell at Hillsboro, ND, and 21 inches fell at Ada, MN.  Many sunday church services and other activities were cancelled.  Most schools were closed on monday, or started late.  A 15 to 20 car pileup occurred on the Interstate 94 bridge between Fargo and Moorhead, with only minor injuries reported.  Because of the snow, many areas only had partial mail service on monday.  Such heavy amounts of snow had not been seen since the record setting winter of 1996/97.  Many counties across Minnesota welcomed the heavy snow, as recent years had been tough for outdoor recreational activities.

A 15 to 20 car pileup occurred on the Interstate 94 bridge between Fargo and Moorhead, with only minor injuries reported.  Because of the snow, many areas only had partial mail service on monday.  Such heavy amounts of snow had not been seen since the record setting winter of 1996/97.  Many counties across Minnesota welcomed the heavy snow, as recent years had been tough for outdoor recreational activities.

An inverted trough extended into the northern plains from an area of surface low pressure over the central plains.  Much of the area received over 6 inches of snow.  A heavy snow band set up from Cooperstown to Mayville (North Dakota) and toward Wadena, Minnesota.  Up to 2 feet of snow fell along this line.  Wind chills of 20 below to 30 below zero also occurred during the day sunday (January 25th).  17 inches of snow fell at Hillsboro, ND, and 21 inches fell at Ada, MN.  Many sunday church services and other activities were cancelled.  Most schools were closed on monday, or started late.  A 15 to 20 car pileup occurred on the Interstate 94 bridge between Fargo and Moorhead, with only minor injuries reported.  Because of the snow, many areas only had partial mail service on monday.  Such heavy amounts of snow had not been seen since the record setting winter of 1996/97.  Many counties across Minnesota welcomed the heavy snow, as recent years had been tough for outdoor recreational activities.

Arctic high pressure built into the northern plains in the wake of the January 24th-26th snowstorm, which dropped heavy snow over all of northwest Minnesota.  With subzero temperatures and wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph, wind chills dropped to 40 below to 65 below zero on the afternoon of the 27th.  Therefore, a wind chill warning was issued from the afternoon of the 27th through the afternoon of the 28th.  Dalton, MN, reported a wind chill of 62 below zero.  Wind speeds dropped off enough that the wind chill warning was not extended beyond the afternoon of the 28th.  However, with the arctic high pressure over the area, extremely cold temperatures abounded.  On the morning of the 27th, Perley fell to 36 below zero.  The coldest morning occurred on the 30th, when the temperature at Fosston fell to 50 degrees below zero, Ada fell to 48 below, Park Rapids fell to 45 below, and Thorhult fell to 43 below.  These were the coldest temperatures recorded during this stretch of subzero weather.  On the morning of the 31st, both Perley and Roseau reported lows of 34 degrees below zero.  Auto repair shops were kept busy jumping stalled cars, installing block heaters, repairing flat tires, and fixing batteries and spark plugs.  Residents around Moran Lake and Two Inlets Lake (near Park Rapids) reported brief power outages.

Strong northwest winds, gusting as high as 40 mph, combined with heavy snow cover to cause widespread blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were frequently below a half mile in open areas. The drifting and low visibilities made travel hazardous, and slippery roads caused by the drifting added to the hazard. Numerous schools either cancelled classes or released students early. Light snow accumulated to less than a half inch, but added to the visibility reduction at times.

Low pressure over Nebraska moved across southern Minnesota during the morning of the 1st bringing a few inches of snowfall accumulation. A second, more powerful storm tracked from Missouri into Illinois early on the 2nd. The second storm dropped moderate to heavy snow across east central and south central Minnesota between the evening of the 1st and the evening of the 2nd. Snowfall totals from both storms ranged from 6 inches along a line from Redwood Falls to Mora to a foot across south central Minnesota (see attached graphic). The highest totals included 13 inches in Faribault and 12 inches at St. James, Albert Lea, and Blue Earth. Behind the second system, temperatures plummeted into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills dropped into the 30s below zero on the morning of the 3rd.

Low pressure tracked over western Minnesota during the evening of the 8th. This storm dropped a narrow band of 6 to 10 inches of snow across Swift and Pope counties. Snowfall totals included 10 inches north of Appleton and 9 inches in Glenwood. In addition, northwest winds gusted up to 20 mph after the snowfall ended on the evening of the 8th causing blowing and drifting on roads and in open areas.

With subzero temperatures and wind speeds of 10 to 20 mph, wind chills dropped to 40 below to 65 below zero on the afternoon of the 27th.  Therefore, a wind chill warning was issued from the afternoon of the 27th through the afternoon of the 28th.  Dalton, MN, reported a wind chill of 62 below zero.  Wind speeds dropped off enough that the wind chill warning was not extended beyond the afternoon of the 28th.  However, with the arctic high pressure over the area, extremely cold temperatures abounded.  On the morning of the 27th, Perley fell to 36 below zero.  The coldest morning occurred on the 30th, when the temperature at Fosston fell to 50 degrees below zero, Ada fell to 48 below, Park Rapids fell to 45 below, and Thorhult fell to 43 below.  These were the coldest temperatures recorded during this stretch of subzero weather.  On the morning of the 31st, both Perley and Roseau reported lows of 34 degrees below zero.  Auto repair shops were kept busy jumping stalled cars, installing block heaters, repairing flat tires, and fixing batteries and spark plugs.  Residents around Moran Lake and Two Inlets Lake (near Park Rapids) reported brief power outages.

An inverted trough extended into the northern plains from an area of surface low pressure over the central plains.  Much of the area received over 6 inches of snow.  A heavy snow band set up from Cooperstown to Mayville (North Dakota) and toward Wadena, Minnesota.  Up to 2 feet of snow fell along this line.  Wind chills of 20 below to 30 below zero also occurred during the day sunday (January 25th).  17 inches of snow fell at Hillsboro, ND, and 21 inches fell at Ada, MN.  Many sunday church services and other activities were cancelled.  Most schools were closed on monday, or started late.  A 15 to 20 car pileup occurred on the Interstate 94 bridge between Fargo and Moorhead, with only minor injuries reported.  Because of the snow, many areas only had partial mail service on monday.  Such heavy amounts of snow had not been seen since the record setting winter of 1996/97.  Many counties across Minnesota welcomed the heavy snow, as recent years had been tough for outdoor recreational activities.

Low pressure over Nebraska moved across southern Minnesota during the morning of the 1st bringing a few inches of snowfall accumulation. A second, more powerful storm tracked from Missouri into Illinois early on the 2nd. The second storm dropped moderate to heavy snow across east central and south central Minnesota between the evening of the 1st and the evening of the 2nd. Snowfall totals from both storms ranged from 6 inches along a line from Redwood Falls to Mora to a foot across south central Minnesota (see attached graphic). The highest totals included 13 inches in Faribault and 12 inches at St. James, Albert Lea, and Blue Earth. Behind the second system, temperatures plummeted into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills dropped into the 30s below zero on the morning of the 3rd.

A surface low over western South Dakota friday morning tracked into eastern South Dakota by late friday morning.  It then tracked northeast along its inverted trough, roughly from Wahpeton, ND, at noon, to Lake of the Woods, MN, by early evening.  Arctic high pressure built in quickly behind the departing low, creating a tight surface pressure gradient and gusty northwest winds.  Wind speeds were steady at 20 to 30 mph with gusts to 35 mph.  Wind speeds were a bit higher across the extreme northern Red River Valley.  At Hallock, MN, winds gusted up to 45 mph.  Blowing snow resulted in visibilities below a half-mile in open areas, and near zero at times in the northern Red River Valley.  Eight inches of snow fell across portions of Roseau, East Marshall, and Northern Beltrami counties, with 4 to 6 inches elsewhere.  Several schools cancelled classes and evening activities on friday.

Strong northwest winds, gusting as high as 40 mph, combined with heavy snow cover to cause widespread blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were frequently below a half mile in open areas. The drifting and low visibilities made travel hazardous, and slippery roads caused by the drifting added to the hazard. Numerous schools either cancelled classes or released students early. Light snow accumulated to less than a half inch, but added to the visibility reduction at times.

Low pressure tracked from eastern Kansas into southern Wisconsin on the morning of the 5th.  This storm dropped wet heavy snow across east central and south central Minnesota between midnight and 10 AM.  The heavy snowfall was responsible for downing scattered trees and power lines which resulted in power outages in some locations. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches.  The 8 inch totals were found in Chanhassen, Chaska, Savage, Lakeville, Montgomery, and Faribault.

An area of surface low pressure tracked near Omaha, NE, with an inverted trough extending northward into the Red River Valley.  Temperatures hovered around and above freezing along the valley and into Minnesota.  Temperatures were a bit cooler to the west of the trough in North Dakota, generally around or below freezing.  The most snow fell where temperatures were the coldest, mostly north or west of Devils Lake, ND.  Starkweather, ND, reported the most snow, 5.5 inches, and Leeds, ND, picked up 5 inches.  The rest of the area received roughly 2 to 5 inches of snow along with about 1/2 inch of sleet or ice.  As the system departed the area, north winds increased to 20 to 35 mph, producing near-zero visibilities in open country.

The rest of the area received roughly 2 to 5 inches of snow along with about 1/2 inch of sleet or ice.  As the system departed the area, north winds increased to 20 to 35 mph, producing near-zero visibilities in open country.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew the roof off a barn and destroyed a machine shed. Two cars, a combine, and a field sprayer in the machine shed were damaged. The winds destroyed a barn on another farm, damaging a tractor and a van inside. The winds blew over a sign anchored in cement, with two tractors being damaged when the sign fell on them. Widespread tree and power line damage was also reported.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree and power line damage. The amount of damage was not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked from eastern Kansas into southern Wisconsin on the morning of the 5th.  This storm dropped wet heavy snow across east central and south central Minnesota between midnight and 10 AM.  The heavy snowfall was responsible for downing scattered trees and power lines which resulted in power outages in some locations. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches.  The 8 inch totals were found in Chanhassen, Chaska, Savage, Lakeville, Montgomery, and Faribault.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime sized hail fell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: [Rain fell on the 27th into the 28th over portions of northwest Minnesota.  The Mayville, ND, radar showed most of the precipitation centered over the Devils Lake basin in North Dakota, with lesser amounts falling across northwest Minnesota.  Exact rainfall amounts were hard to come by, as many field observers did not have their raingauges up yet.  Also, the NDAWN (NDSU) precipitation network was not active.  This network is usually not activated until early April.  Therefore, the only amounts reported were generally from official NWS cooperative observers, who are not a very dense network (usually about 1 per county).  These cooperative observers reported amounts from a quarter inch up to nearly an inch.  0.79 inches was reported at the cooperative weather station in Roseau, MN.  Being late March, soil surface and subsurface temperatures were around and mostly below freezing.  Areas along and north of US Highway 2 still had some snow cover, which was readily apparent on visible satellite imagery in the days leading up to this event.  The warm rain falling on top of snow led to even more water being released.  Snow depths and water equivalents are usually performed by official NWS cooperative observers every monday in the spring, while there is still snow on the ground.  On the previous monday (the 22nd), the Agassiz Refuge (in eastern Marshall county) reported a snow water equivalent of 2.75 inches and Thorhult (in northwest Beltrami county) reported a snow water equivalent of 2.01 inches.  Some melting had occurred in the days leading up to March 27th, but most of this water was still in place as ice or snow.  Snow depths on the 27th ranged from a trace to 6-12 inches from the Agassiz Refuge to Norris Camp (in Lake of the Woods county).  Along with the frozen ground temperatures, river channels in the area still had their winter ice covering left, which led to an increased ice jam threat.  Snow and ice also clogged culverts and drainage ditches.  Temperatures on the 27th were above freezing, mostly in the 40s, while southeast ND rose into the mid 60s.  Temperatures stayed above freezing until the morning of the 29th, but even this was too late to slow the moving water.]  In Kittson county, a river flood warning was issued for the Two Rivers River at Hallock.  Hallock crested just over its moderate (806 feet) flood stage on the 29th.  Crests at Drayton and Pembina along the Red River occurred in early April.  State highway 11 to the Drayton bridge was closed by high water.  In Lancaster, one home had to sandbag due to a closed culvert.  Overall, the county engineer estimated $50,000 in road damages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell between 10 PM CDT on the 29th and 3 AM on the 30th caused widespread flooding of roads, fields, and waterways across Redwood and southern Renville counties.  In Renville County, portions of County Road 15 along the Minnesota River near between Franklin and Fairfax were severely eroded.  A mudslide was reported on County Road 15 near Morton. Several other ditches and gravel roads were eroded across the southern portion of the county.  Buffalo Creek nearly overflowed.  Portions of county roads 4 and 20 were closed by high water.   A total of 8 inches of rain was reported near Franklin.  In Redwood County, significant flooding was reported in Redwood Falls, Wabasso, and Morgan. Eight county roads had to be closed after water flowed over them. In Redwood Falls the Minnesota River crested 2 feet above flood stage.  The Redwood River, the Cottonwood River, and Ramsey Creek also ran high. Ramsey Park and the sway back bridge were closed by high water in addition to nearby County Road 31.  Numerous basements were flooded across the city.  In Morgan, many city residents reported flooded basements and nearby gravel roads were washed out. Farmers outside of town had to replant beans and corn after their fields flooded.  The highest rainfall totals included 8 inches in New Avon Township, 7 inches in Wabasso, and 5 inches in Redwood Falls.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous low pressure system over South Dakota moved into western Minnesota late on the 18th.  This system brought windy conditions to southern Minnesota during the afternoon. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 MPH were reported in several locations south and east of a line from Redwood County to Hennepin County to Chisago County.  Several jurisdictions in this area reported downed trees and power lines, and a handful of structures were also damaged by the high wind gusts.

Strong winds struck a small area of southeast Otter Tail county, first appearing as a large black cloud.  One farm, about 5 miles east of Urbank, was hit particularly hard.  Four forage wagons were moved by the wind, one being flipped over.  A fishhouse was blown 50 feet into a building.  The doors on a quonset were also blown in.

Numerous reports of large hail ranging in size from pennies to as large as golf balls were received from the public, spotters and law enforcement officials.  A few wind gusts around 65 mph were also reported.

Low pressure over Nebraska moved across southern Minnesota during the morning of the 1st bringing a few inches of snowfall accumulation. A second, more powerful storm tracked from Missouri into Illinois early on the 2nd. The second storm dropped moderate to heavy snow across east central and south central Minnesota between the evening of the 1st and the evening of the 2nd. Snowfall totals from both storms ranged from 6 inches along a line from Redwood Falls to Mora to a foot across south central Minnesota (see attached graphic). The highest totals included 13 inches in Faribault and 12 inches at St. James, Albert Lea, and Blue Earth. Behind the second system, temperatures plummeted into the single digits and teens below zero. Wind chills dropped into the 30s below zero on the morning of the 3rd.

Strong northwest winds, gusting as high as 40 mph, combined with heavy snow cover to cause widespread blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were frequently below a half mile in open areas. The drifting and low visibilities made travel hazardous, and slippery roads caused by the drifting added to the hazard. Numerous schools either cancelled classes or released students early. Light snow accumulated to less than a half inch, but added to the visibility reduction at times.

Low pressure tracked from eastern Kansas into southern Wisconsin on the morning of the 5th.  This storm dropped wet heavy snow across east central and south central Minnesota between midnight and 10 AM.  The heavy snowfall was responsible for downing scattered trees and power lines which resulted in power outages in some locations. Snowfall totals ranged from 5 to 8 inches.  The 8 inch totals were found in Chanhassen, Chaska, Savage, Lakeville, Montgomery, and Faribault.

[A surface low tracked across northern South Dakota, southeast North Dakota, and into northern Minnesota.  A strong thermal gradient set up across eastern ND and the northwest quarter of MN.  Temperatures rose into the lower 80s across southeast ND and west central MN (with low 60 dew points) by the afternoon of the 11th.  Afternoon highs on the 11th along the Canadian border only peaked out in the upper 30s and lower 40s.  Strong south-southeast winds surged plenty of moisture into the system.  Severe weather broke out by early evening (11th) over southeast ND and it progressed north and east.  The largest hail and scattered tornadoes formed in the four county corners of Clay, western Becker, western Otter Tail, and Wilkin.  Training thunderstorms along this four county corridor dropped heavy amounts of rain (4-6 inches), causing some flash flooding across the Barnesville, Cormorant, Lawndale, Pelican Rapids, and Detroit Lakes areas.  Streets were flooded and barricaded in the larger towns and lakes rose over docks.  There were also reports of stalled cars due to high water levels on roadways.  As temperatures fell to the middle to upper 20s overnight into the morning of the 12th, accumulating ice and snow occurred in counties along the Canadian border in northeast ND.  One-half to one inch of ice accumulated on exposed surfaces, wrecking havoc on rural electrical lines and trees.  Roughly 1200 customers in Cavalier and Ramsey counties lost power.  As estimated 750 power poles were downed, causing $1.5 million in damages.  Strong winds behind the cold front helped cause a domino-effect in snapping stretches of power poles.  Power restoration efforts took up to 2 weeks for some customers.  Most power pole damage occurred east of highway 1 from from Canada to Fairdale.  An official from Cavalier county estimated 25 percent of the trees in the county were damaged or destroyed.  Emerging crops also were damaged.  Long duration county and river flooding also became an issue across extreme northwest MN, with 3-6 inches of rain also falling in these areas toward midnight on the 11th.  Flood warnings were issued for Roseau and Marshall counties, the Roseau River at Roseau, and the Two Rivers River at Hallock.  The city of Roseau was only hit by record flooding two years ago, in 2002.]

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential downpours fell from a line of thunderstorms that trained over south central Minnesota between the evening of the 8th and the pre-dawn hours of the 9th.  A total of 3.5 to 6.5 inches of rain in 8 hours caused significant flooding in Blue Earth and Nicollet counties and minor flooding in Le Sueur and Waseca counties.  An estimated 5 percent of corn and soybean crops were lost to flooding across Blue Earth and Nicollet counties during this event. In Blue Earth County,  rainfall totals included 7.05 inches in Lime Township just north of Mankato, 6.51 inches at Mankato Municipal Airport, 6.24 inches in Mankato just west of MSU, 5.7 inches five miles southwest of Lake Crystal, and 4.59 inches at South Central Technical College in Mankato.  Area storm drains and culverts were unable to keep up with the deluge of rain, especially in the Mankato, Madison Lake, and Lake Crystal areas.  Water backed up into retaining ponds, fields, numerous basements, roads, waterways and other low lying areas.  Third Avenue in Lime Township just north of Mankato lost 30 feet of roadway after a large culvert was washed out.  Saturated soil led to a mudslide on Warren Street near MSU.  A retention pond at Country Club Estates in Mankato overflowed into nearby homes with walkout basements. Flooding was also reported near the hospital in hilltop Mankato.   In Nicollet County,  2.5 inches was recorded in St. Peter.  A portion of a 50-foot-tall hillside gave way along the southbound lanes of Highway 169 between Mankato and St. Peter around 3 AM on the 9th.  This caused a foot of mud and downed vegetation to pour across all 4 lanes of the highway.  The northbound side of the road remained closed through Noon on the 9th but the fear of more mudslides kept the southbound side of the highway closed through the 10th.  In addition, a large mudslide which included trees and vegetation from a steep hillside occurred along Lake Street in North Mankato on the 10th. A retention pond near Dakota Meadows Middle School overflowed onto Howard Drive in North Mankato.  Significant flooding was also reported in hilltop North Mankato and Center  Street and McKinley Avenue in lower North Mankato. Two small bridges south of St. Peter were damaged by swiftly moving water. In Waseca County, 2.46 inches of rain was reported in Waseca. An estimated 5 inches of rain fell over the Janesville area.  Up to 80 basements were flooded and several lawns and streets were covered by water across town.  Janesville's city park and golf course were also under water.  In Le Sueur County, 4.7 inches was recorded at New Prague.

The northbound side of the road remained closed through Noon on the 9th but the fear of more mudslides kept the southbound side of the highway closed through the 10th.  In addition, a large mudslide which included trees and vegetation from a steep hillside occurred along Lake Street in North Mankato on the 10th. A retention pond near Dakota Meadows Middle School overflowed onto Howard Drive in North Mankato.  Significant flooding was also reported in hilltop North Mankato and Center  Street and McKinley Avenue in lower North Mankato. Two small bridges south of St. Peter were damaged by swiftly moving water. In Waseca County, 2.46 inches of rain was reported in Waseca. An estimated 5 inches of rain fell over the Janesville area.  Up to 80 basements were flooded and several lawns and streets were covered by water across town.  Janesville's city park and golf course were also under water.  In Le Sueur County, 4.7 inches was recorded at New Prague.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water remained high along waterways, low lying areas, and roads through the morning of the 9th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential downpours fell from a line of thunderstorms that trained over south central Minnesota between the evening of the 8th and the pre-dawn hours of the 9th.  A total of 3.5 to 6.5 inches of rain in 8 hours caused significant flooding in Blue Earth and Nicollet counties and minor flooding in Le Sueur and Waseca counties.  An estimated 5 percent of corn and soybean crops were lost to flooding across Blue Earth and Nicollet counties during this event. In Blue Earth County,  rainfall totals included 7.05 inches in Lime Township just north of Mankato, 6.51 inches at Mankato Municipal Airport, 6.24 inches in Mankato just west of MSU, 5.7 inches five miles southwest of Lake Crystal, and 4.59 inches at South Central Technical College in Mankato.  Area storm drains and culverts were unable to keep up with the deluge of rain, especially in the Mankato, Madison Lake, and Lake Crystal areas.  Water backed up into retaining ponds, fields, numerous basements, roads, waterways and other low lying areas.  Third Avenue in Lime Township just north of Mankato lost 30 feet of roadway after a large culvert was washed out.  Saturated soil led to a mudslide on Warren Street near MSU.  A retention pond at Country Club Estates in Mankato overflowed into nearby homes with walkout basements. Flooding was also reported near the hospital in hilltop Mankato.   In Nicollet County,  2.5 inches was recorded in St. Peter. This caused a foot of mud and downed vegetation to pour across all 4 lanes of the highway. In addition, a large mudslide which included trees and vegetation from a steep hillside occurred along Lake Street in North Mankato on the 10th. A retention pond near Dakota Meadows Middle School overflowed onto Howard Drive in North Mankato.  Significant flooding was also reported in hilltop North Mankato and Center  Street and McKinley Avenue in lower North Mankato. Two small bridges south of St. Peter were damaged by swiftly moving water. In Waseca County, 2.46 inches of rain was reported in Waseca. An estimated 5 inches of rain fell over the Janesville area. EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was relayed by HAM Radio operators near Ellendale.
A farm off Route 28 sustained significant damage. A storage bin was blown onto a field. Windows were blown out of a hog barn.  A farmhouse lost shingles and trees on the property were downed.
A tornado crossed into Yellow Medicine County from Lincoln County just west of Porter.  It dissipated shortly after with little if any damage reported.  The time was estimated.
Quarter sized hail was reported near the intersection of Radisson and Bunker Lake roads.
Two grain bins were damaged.  A shed and a tree were downed.
Field crops were destroyed by wind driven hail.
Tree tops were snapped.

One machine shed was destroyed and another was heavily damaged on a farm along Highway 15.  A Quonset hut was also shifted off its foundation.  Six power poles along Highway 15 near the farm were downed.
Two trees were downed onto York Road east of Underwood Avenue.
A construction trailer was flipped on the southwest side of town. Two people inside the trailer were injured. Trees were downed in the vicinity.
Numerous corn and bean crops were shredded by hail.  Some of the worst damage was found in a two to three mile area east of Lake Marion.
A cluster of trees was downed.
The nickel sized hail stripped leaves and small branches from trees.
Large trees were downed.

Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including numerous trees blown down. One pine tree penetrated the roof of a house. The house suffered other wind damage, including to the porch. The garage was heavily damaged and farm equipment was damaged.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including 8 inch diameter branches blown down. The winds also blew shingles off roofs, and blew the back door from a barn.
Large branches were downed.
Several citizens called 911 to report a funnel cloud.
A tree was blown down along Beam Avenue.
Penny sized hail fell between Melrose and Belgrade.
A MNDOT automated wind sensor recorded a gust of 59 MPH.
Penny sized hail fell 1/2 mile south of Highway 15 near Delwood Road.
A tree was downed.
A few power lines were downed. The time was estimated.
A MNDOT automated wind sensor recorded a gust of 61 MPH.
A tree was snapped.  The time was estimated.
Rainfall of 2 to 4 inches in a short time flooded numerous roads and a few basements.

Rainfall of 2 to 5 inches caused flooding of basements, fields, and many roads. Several roads were blocked by high water, including state highway 30 and U.S. highway 59. The basement flooding was most noted in Slayton and in the Lake Shetek area.
Rainfall of around 3 inches caused flooding of several roads and basements in Pipestone.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage. The amount of damage was not known.
Rainfall of 2 to 5 inches caused flooding of roads, fields, and basements. Basement flooding was noted especially in Tracy. A Tracy veterinary clinic was flooded and heavily damaged.
Large hail covered the ground.
Runoff from heavy rain caused flooding of Pipestone Creek, with farmland and other fields flooded.

A tornado destroyed a storage shed, silo, and outbuildings on a farm. The tornado also caused shingle and gutter damage to a house on the farm. On another farm, two corn bins were destroyed and a pole barn was damaged. Two large trees were blown down at the second farm, one of the trees damaging the corner of a house when it fell. The tornado was visible from long distances and photographed from several locations.
Large hail stripped leaves off trees, and caused an unknown amount of crop damage.

Large hail covered the ground and caused significant damage to corn, soybean, and other small grain crops. The amount of damage was unknown, because many of the early stage crops were expected to grow back, and others could be replanted.

Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree damage. Trees as large as a foot and a half in diameter were blown down, along with numerous limbs and branches. Several roads were blocked by tree debris. The winds also caused damage to power lines.

Thunderstorm winds destroyed two hog barns and a feed mill, and damaged a grain bin. One thousand hogs died of suffocation as a result of the damage. The owner's house on the other side of a nearby road was undamaged.
Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines near state highway 30 just east of the South Dakota state line.
Thunderstorm winds caused tree damage, including several trees blown down. The amount of damage was not known.
A tornado destroyed a 48 foot grain bin. The tornado also caused widespread tree damage, including several trees blown down.
Shingles were blown off a roof.
6-8 inch diameter trees were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: [See the event description under the 5/11/04 flash flood event for Wilkin county, Lawndale.]  For northwest Minnesota, about a half-inch of wet snow and over 4 inches of rain fell, which resulted in county-wide and river flooding in Roseau county.  The Roseau River at Roseau crested around 20.5 feet on May 14th (just over its major flood stage of 20.0 feet).  This is under the maximum stage the Roseau River reached in 2002, but still resulted in big problems for the city of Roseau.  A call went out for sandbaggers, so the Roseau High School let its nearly 600 students out to help.  Many roads in Roseau were flooded, mostly on the west side of town south of highway 11.  The U.S. Army Corps of Engineers started efforts to reinforce the dike, which was a piecemeal dike hastily thrown together years before.  Some roads in the Wannaska area also were flooded.  State highway 11 also had about a foot of water flowing over it 2 miles southeast of Warroad.
[See the event description under the 5/11/04 flash flood event for Wilkin county, Lawndale.]  Several rural roads were flooded by the heavy rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado caused no reported damage.
Large hail covered the ground.
Large hail covered old highway 16 and nearby fields.
A MNDOT RWIS sensor reported a 58 mph wind gust.
The AWOS at the Thief River Falls airport reported a 58 mph wind gust.
A MNDOT RWIS sensor reported a 58 mph wind gust.
A MNDOT RWIS sensor reported a 63 mph wind gust.

The tornado demolished 4 structures.  One of the structures, a 30x40 foot machine shed, was swept from its foundation and thrown into the adjacent fields.  Tools from the shed were driven into the banks of a nearby highway.  Other debris was spread over a mile away.  Many trees were torn up.
A brief touchdown was reported.
Dime sized hail fell at Deer Run Golf Course.
Penny sized hail was reported at the intersection of routes 36 and 120.
Six inch diameter trees were downed.

The Two Rivers River crested in Hallock around 808.0 feet between May 16th and 18th, over its 806.0 foot moderate flood stage.

Three funnels formed and one briefly touched down.
Large branches were downed.
Heavy debris was reported in the air near the county maintenance building between Norwood and Cologne along Route 212.
Power lines were downed.
Nine power poles along County Road 5 and 167th Ave. were snapped. The time was estimated.
An automated weather sensor recorded a wind gust of 60 MPH.
Trees were downed at the intersection of routes 212 and 101.  The time was estimated.

Trees were downed in Plymouth, Orono, Independence, Crystal, St. Anthony, and Rogers.  Sailboats were capsized and docks were damaged at Medicine Lake in Plymouth. A flat roof was peeled up on a building in Brooklyn Center. A tree fell through a roof in Crystal.
Penny sized hail fell in St. Anthony and at Lake Nokomis.
Dime sized hail fell west of town.
Large branches were snapped off.
A wind gust of 63 MPH was recorded by an automated weather sensor.
Enough nickel sized hail fell to shovel at Swan Lake.
A large tree was downed onto Judson Bottom Road.  The time was estimated.
A small shed was destroyed.
Nickel to golf ball sized hail was reported.
A garage under construction on 113th Ave. NE (Route 10) was damaged. The time was estimated.
Dime to golf ball sized hail covered the ground.
A wind gust of 66 MPH was recorded by an automated weather sensor.  Trees were downed at the intersection of Portland and 5th avenues and on a house in the northeast portion of town.
Penny sized hail fell in Sumter and at Lake Marion.  The time was estimated.

A wind gust of 72 MPH was recorded by an automated sensor at Holman Field at 6:00 PM CDT. Numerous trees and power lines were downed from St. Anthony Park to the State Fairgrounds to the State Capitol including the southern portion of Como Park and the Midway area. A handful of buildings and signs at the fairgrounds were damaged. Four homes, a large porch, a fence, and a car near Como Park were significantly damaged by felled trees. In downtown about 60 windows were broken on buildings and skyways and a large billboard was downed onto power lines.  Two of four clock faces were damaged on top of Landmark Center. The high winds also caused two barges to break loose on the Mississippi River and bump into the Robert Street Bridge.
An automated weather sensor recorded a wind gust of 60 MPH. The time was estimated.

A tornado touched down near Preusse Lake just east of Big Lake Industrial Park. It sucked up water as it crossed the lake then after hitting land it downed trees near a home on 172nd Street.  The twister crossed over 172nd Street then went into the Bruggeman residential development before dissipating. A home on Sundstrom Hill lost shingles and soffit and a flag pole was downed.
A few trees and power lines were downed.
Three trees were snapped.
A hoop greenhouse building and the trees and shrubs inside the structure were damaged at a nursery.  The time was estimated.
A house was damaged and an attached garage was destroyed along 76th Lane.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain that fell between 10 PM CDT on the 29th and 3 AM on the 30th caused widespread flooding of roads, fields, and waterways across Redwood and southern Renville counties.  In Renville County, portions of County Road 15 along the Minnesota River near between Franklin and Fairfax were severely eroded.  A mudslide was reported on County Road 15 near Morton. Several other ditches and gravel roads were eroded across the southern portion of the county.  Buffalo Creek nearly overflowed.  Portions of county roads 4 and 20 were closed by high water.   A total of 8 inches of rain was reported near Franklin.  In Redwood County, significant flooding was reported in Redwood Falls, Wabasso, and Morgan. Eight county roads had to be closed after water flowed over them. In Redwood Falls the Minnesota River crested 2 feet above flood stage.  The Redwood River, the Cottonwood River, and Ramsey Creek also ran high. Ramsey Park and the sway back bridge were closed by high water in addition to nearby County Road 31.  Numerous basements were flooded across the city.  In Morgan, many city residents reported flooded basements and nearby gravel roads were washed out. Farmers outside of town had to replant beans and corn after their fields flooded.  The highest rainfall totals included 8 inches in New Avon Township, 7 inches in Wabasso, and 5 inches in Redwood Falls.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads, fields, and waterways, including the Redwood and Minnesota rivers, remained flooded through the evening of the 31st.
A pontoon boat was blown off its lift.
A fish house was blown over and rolled 50 feet.
Dime sized hail fell in Eden Valley.
Walnut sized hail was reported at Sugar Lake.
A 10 inch diameter tree was snapped in half.
The time was estimated.
Trees were downed and a pop-up trailer was opened and rolled in town. A shed 2 miles southwest of town was damaged.
Two turkey barns were slightly damaged along Route 23 near Long Lake.
Trees were downed onto Kale Aveunue. The time was estimated.
An automated weather sensor recorded a wind gust of 68 MPH. A tree was downed onto Jefferson Street SE.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large hail up to baseball size fell for about ten minutes. The hail damaged vehicles and windows.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weak tornado was reported by trained spotters just north of the intersection of Highway 10 and County Road 6. It dissipated northeast of town a short time later with little if any damage reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Law enforcement officials reported several trees were blown down by winds estimated around 60 mph.

There was widespread wind damage from west of Pequot Lakes and across the northern part of Crow Wing County from Pequot Lakes, Jenkins, Cross Lake to Emily and back into Cass County at Outing. Numerous trees were snapped and uprooted and 8,000 people lost electrical power. There was major roof damage to businesses in Pequot Lakes and Cross Lake.  Homes suffered roof damage from falling trees at Breezy Point.  Numerous vehicles were damaged from falling trees and branches. The worst damage was in the Cross Lake and Ideal townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds knocked down trees and power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed the Minnesota-Iowa state line just southwest of Le Roy (Mower County) causing considerable damage.  One home right on the state line was completely destroyed, but there were no injuries or deaths.  The tornado was approximately 150 yards wide at times and apparently reached F3 damage intensity right on the state line around 6:05 p.m.  The tornado then started to weaken, but did heavy damage to a grain company (Koch Industries)(F2 damage) about 2 miles west of Le Roy and continued north, crossing Highway 56 just before 6:10 p.m. The tornado lifted approximately 4 miles northwest of Le Roy in open fields.  There were also brief tornado touch downs (all rated F0) near Dexter (Mower County) and Le Roy, as well as areas along the Mower-Fillmore County line west of Spring Valley (Fillmore County).

An upper level low pressure system parked near Hudson Bay helped produce unusually cold weather for mid June.  On the morning of the 23rd, the NDAWN station 11 miles south of Roseau reported 32 degrees and the NDAWN station 6 miles southwest of Warren reported 32 degrees.  NWS cooperative observers reported 30 degrees at Thorhult, 32 degrees at Norris Camp, 33 degrees at Agassiz National Wildlife Refuge, and 34 degrees 2 miles southeast of Karlstad.  On the morning of the 24th, the NDAWN station 3 miles west-southwest of Mavie reported 37 degrees and the NDAWN station 7 miles west of Greenbush reported 38 degrees.  These temperatures were cold enough to produce patchy frost, with mid June being well into the growing season.  Some sensitive crops, especially over northwest Minnesota, were unable to recover.  Record lows were set on the 24th at Grand Forks and Fargo (ND).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water overflowed across County Road 13.  Crops were damaged by flood waters near Brownton.  Up to 3 inches of rain fell southwest of Glencoe.

[See the May 2004 Storm Data report for additional information on this flood event.  3 to 6 inches of rain fell along a Cando to Valley City to Hankinson (ND) line on May 29-31, and produced a long duration flood event for North Dakota counties along this line.  The flooding began as flash flooding (over southeast ND) on the 29th of May, and then transitioned to county-wide flooding (in ND) that continued into early June.  The rains on May 29-31 also contributed to high river levels across portions of northwest Minnesota, and river flood warnings at Oslo and Hallock (MN).]  A river flood warning was issued for the Two Rivers River at Hallock on the afternoon of May 30th.  The Two Rivers River at Hallock crested around 806.8 feet MSL on the 1st of June, over its 806 foot MSL moderate flood stage.  River levels dropped enough that the river flood warning was cancelled on the morning of the 4th.

On the morning of the 24th, the NDAWN station 3 miles west-southwest of Mavie reported 37 degrees and the NDAWN station 7 miles west of Greenbush reported 38 degrees. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inch diameter branches were downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tornado crossed the Minnesota-Iowa state line just southwest of Le Roy (Mower County) causing considerable damage.  One home right on the state line was completely destroyed, but there were no injuries or deaths.  The tornado was approximately 150 yards wide at times and apparently reached F3 damage intensity right on the state line around 6:05 p.m.  The tornado then started to weaken, but did heavy damage to a grain company (Koch Industries)(F2 damage) about 2 miles west of Le Roy and continued north, crossing Highway 56 just before 6:10 p.m. The tornado lifted approximately 4 miles northwest of Le Roy in open fields.  There were also brief tornado touch downs (all rated F0) near Dexter (Mower County) and Le Roy, as well as areas along the Mower-Fillmore County line west of Spring Valley (Fillmore County).
Law enforcement officials and spotters reported a couple of weak tornadoes touched down briefly, but no damage occurred.

River levels dropped enough that the river flood warning was cancelled on the morning of the 5th.

Trees up to one foot in diameter were downed.  One felled tree damaged a home. A temporary greenhouse was also damaged.

A tornado touched just southwest of the intersection of Highway 33 and Highway 6 about 3 miles northwest of Freeborn.  It moved northeast on the ground for 4 miles before lifting just east of Highway 10 about 5 miles north northeast of Freeborn.  The tornado caused damage to a number of farm buildings along its path.  On 632nd and 640th avenues, trees were downed.  On 655th Avenue, 3 foot diameter trees were downed and a house sustained minor damage. On 660th Avenue, large trees were downed, power poles were tilted, three grain bins were damaged, and minor damage was reported to buildings.
HAM Radio operators relayed a spotter report of a funnel cloud halfway down to the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: 3 to 6 inches of rain fell in three hours, which caused flash flooding.  Law enforcement officials reported water and mudslides over Highway 52 between Fountain and Preston.

Rainfall amounts of 2 to 4 inches in less than two hours caused flash flooding.  Hardest hit was the city of Rochester (Olmsted County), where law enforcement officials reported numerous basements and streets were flooded.  Several motorists were stranded in their vehicles due to high water.  In fact, water was up to the doors and windshields of some cars.  A ten year old boy was rescued from Cascade Creek when he attempted to cross the swollen creek on a surf board.

Thunderstorm winds destroyed a grain elevator and several grain bins. The winds also damaged roofs and siding on several homes and businesses.  There was widespread tree damage including several trees blown down. Homes, businesses, and vehicles sustained additional damage from falling or flying tree debris, and debris blocked several roads. Power lines were blown down, resulting in power outages. The winds caused severe crop damage in the area, but the amount of crop damage could not be determined.
Golf ball sized hail shredded over 1000 acres of soybean and corn crops in St. Johns Township southwest of Pennock.  Two homes in the area suffered broken windows and damage to shingles and siding.

Trees and power poles were downed in the Knife Lake area.  A home and a handful of camper trailers were damaged. Boats were also flipped on the lake. In addition, many large trees and power poles were downed from Peace into Whited Townships.
A downed tree barely missed a house 2 miles south of Rose City.
Penny to nickel sized hail flattened a garden and chipped paint off a home.

Large hail fell in a swath from the north central to southeast parts of Rock County. Towns affected included Hardwick, Luverne, and Kanaranzi. The hail broke windows and damaged vehicles in Luverne. The hail also caused widespread damage to corn and soybean crops along its path with leaves stripped and stalks bruised.
A brief tornado track was found.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential downpours on the evening of the 5th caused water to rush across rural county roads and basements to flood south and east of Albert Lea. Roads in Albert Lea, including Highway 65 just south of town, were also covered by water. Some crops in Rome Township had to be replanted after flood waters ran through fields. Rainfall totals included 5.20 inches between London and Myrtle, 4.5 inches in Twin Lakes, and 3.7 inches in Albert Lea.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike started a fire that destroyed a storage tank holding 60,000 gallons of heavy industrial oil at a petroleum refinery.  A second storage tank was damaged.
A 10-inch diameter tree and numerous branches were downed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2 to 6 inches over the northern and eastern parts of Murray County flooded numerous roads, and washed out some township roads. Residents in the Lake Shetek area were especially affected, with high water flooding roads to many homes. At least 50 thousand acres of cropland were flooded, with some crops washed away completely. The amount of crop damage was not known, due to the uncertainties in both crop recovery and in the ability to replant. Other lowlands were flooded, including a golf course in Slayton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Up to 6 inches of rain caused flooding of roads, fields, and other low areas, including a golf course in Windom. Flooding of basements was reported in Windom and Mountain Lake.
Lightning struck and destroyed a large tree. The lightning also damaged a computer and a television set in the adjoining house.
Power lines were blown down.
Quarter sized hail fell at an amusement park.

This tornado crossed the Red River into Minnesota (it originated southeast of Wild Rice, ND) and moved east before lifting just west of Rustad.  Many trees were twisted or snapped off along the tornado path.   Several farm buildings were damaged one mile west of Rustad.  One wooden grain bin was moved about 6 feet off its foundation and the door of a 50x150 foot storage shed was blown in and pushed halfway across the shed.

Rainfall of up to four and a half inches on saturated ground caused widespread flooding of streets, roads, and other low areas including farmland. Towns affected included Rushmore, Worthington and Brewster, with numerous basements flooded. The amount of damage was not known.

Large hail damaged crops. The amount of damage was not known.

Intermittent tree damage occurred from the east side of Lake Lizzie to Dent.  Power outages occurred from trees falling on power lines.  On West McDonald Lake, a 30 foot mobile home was overturned against an oak tree that was split in half.  A tree also fell on a boat, destroying it.  At Spirit Lake, 20 trees were damaged at a resort, some falling on resort cabins.  A dock was also damaged.  At Hoffman Lake, property owners reportedly saw the tornado coming across the lake.  Many trees fell on homes or other property.
Power lines were blown down at the intersection of county roads 108 and 85.
The tornado broke off a few small trees near Marion Lake.

Large hail fell in a swath from west central to south central Pipestone County. Towns affected included Pipestone and Trosky. The hail caused significant crop damage along its path, including stripping the leaves off corn and soybean plants and bruising the stems. A large loss of yield was expected in spite of some expected recovery of damaged crops.

Along its rural path, crops and trees were damaged and a shed was downed.
A funnel cloud was spotted by the Pemberton Fire Department just south of town.
Small buildings sustained roof damage and some trees were downed in Section 33 of Iosco Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system parked near Hudson Bay helped produce unusually cold weather for mid June.  On the morning of the 23rd, the NDAWN station 11 miles south of Roseau reported 32 degrees and the NDAWN station 6 miles southwest of Warren reported 32 degrees.  NWS cooperative observers reported 30 degrees at Thorhult, 32 degrees at Norris Camp, 33 degrees at Agassiz National Wildlife Refuge, and 34 degrees 2 miles southeast of Karlstad.  On the morning of the 24th, the NDAWN station 3 miles west-southwest of Mavie reported 37 degrees and the NDAWN station 7 miles west of Greenbush reported 38 degrees.  These temperatures were cold enough to produce patchy frost, with mid June being well into the growing season.  Some sensitive crops, especially over northwest Minnesota, were unable to recover.  Record lows were set on the 24th at Grand Forks and Fargo (ND).

Heavy rainfall across the Straight River basin upstream of Owatonna on the 9th caused high water to flow through much of Steele County between the 10th and the 12th.  Moderate flooding along the Straight River led to several road closures across the county including County Road 18 and 22nd Avenue Southwest.  In addition, 20 basements were flooded in Owatonna, mainly in the Crossroads development. Two families were displaced by flood damage and  were sheltered by the Red Cross.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed onto Highway 71.
Trees were downed.
A power line was downed onto Highway 70.  The time was estimated.
Ponding of water was reported on Highway 47 three miles north of Ogilvie, and on Highway 70 and County Road 2 in the southern portion of the county.
A dozen trees were downed throughout the city.
A funnel cloud was reported by law enforcement.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado caused no reported damage.
Nickel sized hail fell across the eastern portion of the county.
A 69 MPH gust was recorded by a MNDOT automated weather sensor.
Large branches were downed.
A tree was blown down.

Hundreds of trees and power lines were downed and a handful of buildings were damaged across the northwest portion of the county.  The hardest hit areas included the communities of Annandale, Corinna Township, Maple Lake, St. Michael, Clearwater, and South Haven.  Significant tree damage was also reported at Clearwater Lake, Pleasant Lake, Lake Sylvia, Schroeder County Park, Camp Friendship, and Lake Maria State Park.  Highway 24 near Clearwater Lake was blocked by downed trees, and boats and docks on the lake were damaged.  A wind gust of 64 MPH was recorded in Maple Lake at 1:35 AM CDT. A gust of 52 MPH was recorded in Cokato and a gust of 47 MPH was recorded in Annandale at 1:30 AM CDT.

A number of trees and power lines were downed onto yards and structures in the Dassel, Darwin, Knapp, and Lake Jennie areas. The Lake Washington area was also hit hard by downed trees and power lines which damaged several vehicles and structures.  High wind also flipped boats and docks and damaged a sunroom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential downpours fell from a line of thunderstorms that trained over south central Minnesota between the evening of the 8th and the pre-dawn hours of the 9th.  A total of 3.5 to 6.5 inches of rain in 8 hours caused significant flooding in Blue Earth and Nicollet counties and minor flooding in Le Sueur and Waseca counties.  An estimated 5 percent of corn and soybean crops were lost to flooding across Blue Earth and Nicollet counties during this event. In Blue Earth County,  rainfall totals included 7.05 inches in Lime Township just north of Mankato, 6.51 inches at Mankato Municipal Airport, 6.24 inches in Mankato just west of MSU, 5.7 inches five miles southwest of Lake Crystal, and 4.59 inches at South Central Technical College in Mankato.  Area storm drains and culverts were unable to keep up with the deluge of rain, especially in the Mankato, Madison Lake, and Lake Crystal areas.  Water backed up into retaining ponds, fields, numerous basements, roads, waterways and other low lying areas.  Third Avenue in Lime Township just north of Mankato lost 30 feet of roadway after a large culvert was washed out.  Saturated soil led to a mudslide on Warren Street near MSU.  A retention pond at Country Club Estates in Mankato overflowed into nearby homes with walkout basements. Flooding was also reported near the hospital in hilltop Mankato.   In Nicollet County,  2.5 inches was recorded in St. Peter.  A portion of a 50-foot-tall hillside gave way along the southbound lanes of Highway 169 between Mankato and St. Peter around 3 AM on the 9th.  This caused a foot of mud and downed vegetation to pour across all 4 lanes of the highway.  The northbound side of the road remained closed through Noon on the 9th but the fear of more mudslides kept the southbound side of the highway closed through the 10th.  In addition, a large mudslide which included trees and vegetation from a steep hillside occurred along Lake Street in North Mankato on the 10th. A retention pond near Dakota Meadows Middle School overflowed onto Howard Drive in North Mankato.  Significant flooding was also reported in hilltop North Mankato and Center  Street and McKinley Avenue in lower North Mankato. Two small bridges south of St. Peter were damaged by swiftly moving water. In Waseca County, 2.46 inches of rain was reported in Waseca. An estimated 5 inches of rain fell over the Janesville area.  Up to 80 basements were flooded and several lawns and streets were covered by water across town.  Janesville's city park and golf course were also under water.  In Le Sueur County, 4.7 inches was recorded at New Prague.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 5.4 inches of rain was recorded at Belle Plaine.
